network.overall_strategy: The network implements two major literature-supported routes to arginine: (1) the Sutherland/Patel cyanosulfidic pathway, which proceeds from acetylene and HCN through acrylonitrile, β-aminopropionitrile, and UV-driven Kiliani-Fischer homologation to yield the arginine α-aminonitrile, with two sub-routes (direct guanidination vs. hemiaminal 37 trapping); and (2) the ornithine guanidination route, where ornithine is synthesized via HCN oligomer hydrolysis or reductive homologation of β-aminopropionitrile, then converted to arginine by reaction with cyanamide. Hydrothermal environments provide core C1 feedstocks (formate, formaldehyde, HCN) and cyanamide (via urea dehydration). Surface environments drive cyanosulfidic Cu(I)-catalyzed C-C coupling and UV photoredox chemistry. Biochemical environments host hydrolysis and guanidination. Five hub molecules (HCN, cyanamide, β-aminopropionitrile, ornithine, arginine α-aminonitrile) create dense interconnections and convergent pathways.

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, serpentinization-derived H₂

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ adsorbs onto greigite surface and is reduced by H₂ through a surface-bound formate intermediate. The [Fe₄S₄] cluster analog in greigite facilitates two-electron transfer to CO₂.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate has been demonstrated experimentally under simulated alkaline vent conditions (Preiner et al., 2020; Roldan et al., 2015). Greigite is structurally analogous to biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters and is a product of serpentinization.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite
reactions.mechanism: Formate undergoes further reduction on magnetite surfaces via Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry, producing formaldehyde through a two-electron reduction. Magnetite provides the surface and redox environment for partial reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis on magnetite and other iron oxide/sulfide surfaces can reduce C1 oxygenates to more reduced species including formaldehyde and methanol (McCollom & Seewald, 2007; He et al., 2019). Magnetite is abundant in serpentinization zones.

reactions.name: Formaldehyde amination to HCN on pyrite

conditions.other: High-temperature hydrothermal zone; dehydration of formamide intermediate

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with ammonia to form formamide (HCONH₂) as an intermediate, which then undergoes thermal dehydration on pyrite surfaces at elevated temperatures to produce HCN. Pyrite surface activates C-O bond cleavage.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide is a well-established prebiotic precursor that forms readily from formaldehyde and ammonia. Its thermal decomposition to HCN at elevated temperatures on mineral surfaces is documented (Saladino et al., 2012; Ferus et al., 2015). HCN is also produced in abundance by atmospheric photochemistry and impact events, providing an additional exogenous source.

reactions.name: Urea formation from ammonia and CO₂

conditions.other: High pressure favors ammonium carbamate formation; mackinawite surface facilitates dehydration

reactions.mechanism: NH₃ and CO₂ react to form ammonium carbamate (NH₂COONH₄), which undergoes dehydration to urea on mackinawite mineral surfaces. High pressure and moderate temperature drive the equilibrium toward urea.
reactions.reasoning: The industrial Bosch-Meiser process forms urea from NH₃ and CO₂ via ammonium carbamate at high T and P. Prebiotic analogs at hydrothermal conditions have been discussed by Holm & Andersson (2005). Mineral surfaces lower the kinetic barrier for carbamate dehydration. Urea has been detected in interstellar ices and is considered a plausible prebiotic species (Remijan et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Thermal dehydration of urea to cyanamide

conditions.other: Dry-down evaporitic conditions; UV irradiation enhances decomposition on TiO₂ surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Urea undergoes thermal dehydration upon drying, eliminating water to yield cyanamide. TiO₂ photocatalysis and montmorillonite surface adsorption facilitate the elimination. UV exposure on TiO₂ accelerates the process.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decomposition of urea produces cyanamide, biuret, and cyanuric acid depending on conditions (Schaber et al., 2004). Cyanamide is the kinetic product at moderate temperatures. Cyanamide is widely invoked as a prebiotically available reagent in the Sutherland cyanosulfidic framework (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson et al., 2018) and is essential for phosphate activation and guanidination reactions.

reactions.name: Cu(I)-catalyzed cross-coupling of acetylene and HCN to acrylonitrile

conditions.other: Anoxic aqueous solution; Cu(I) ions from dissolution of cuprite in slightly acidic conditions; Sb₂S₃ as sulfide mineral co-catalyst

reactions.mechanism: Cu(I) ions coordinate acetylene, activating it toward nucleophilic addition by cyanide. The copper-acetylide intermediate undergoes HCN insertion to produce acrylonitrile via a vinylcopper species that eliminates Cu(I) upon protonolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated that Cu(I)-catalyzed hydrocyanation of acetylene produces acrylonitrile under mild aqueous conditions. This reaction initiates the cyanosulfidic synthesis of amino acid precursors, establishing the initial C3 skeleton needed for the arginine side chain. Copper minerals would be available in volcanic/hydrothermal surface environments.

reactions.name: Conjugate addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile

conditions.other: Neutral to slightly basic pH; montmorillonite concentrates reactants and lowers activation barrier via surface catalysis

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia undergoes 1,4-conjugate (Michael) addition to the electron-poor double bond of acrylonitrile, producing β-aminopropionitrile. The reaction proceeds readily at room temperature in aqueous ammonia solutions.
reactions.reasoning: The Michael addition of NH₃ to acrylonitrile is a well-known reaction in organic chemistry and is thermodynamically favorable under aqueous conditions. Patel et al. (2015) include this step in the cyanosulfidic pathway to amino acids. Montmorillonite surfaces facilitate the reaction by concentrating organics and NH₃ through adsorption (Ferris, 2005).

reactions.name: Guanidination of β-aminopropionitrile with cyanamide

conditions.other: Slightly alkaline pH; partial conversion characteristic of systems chemistry mixtures

reactions.mechanism: The primary amine of β-aminopropionitrile attacks the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide, forming a guanidine linkage through nucleophilic addition followed by proton transfer. The reaction is relatively inefficient, yielding a mixture of guanidinated and unreacted starting material—a feature that is productive in the systems chemistry context.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) describe the guanidination of β-aminopropionitrile by cyanamide as a key step in the cyanosulfidic arginine precursor pathway. The partial conversion is noted as 'relatively inefficient,' but the systems chemistry approach productively utilizes both the guanidinated product and the unreacted β-aminopropionitrile in parallel downstream pathways.

reactions.name: Kiliani-Fischer homologation of guanidinated nitrile to arginine α-aminonitrile

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm); anoxic conditions; Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycling drives the photoreduction steps; H₂S as reductant and sulfur source

reactions.mechanism: The guanidinated nitrile undergoes UV-driven Kiliani-Fischer homologation: (1) photoreduction of the nitrile to an aldehyde with H₂S via Cu-mediated electron transfer, (2) cyanohydrin formation with HCN, (3) sulfidolysis to α-hydroxythioamide with H₂S, (4) photodeoxygenation under UV to thioamide, (5) final amination with NH₃ and HCN to yield the arginine α-aminonitrile. Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycling provides the redox equivalents throughout.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated the complete Kiliani-Fischer-type homologation pathway for carbon chain extension under cyanosulfidic conditions. The UV-driven Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling, combined with H₂S as both reductant and protecting group source, enables stepwise chain elongation while preserving the guanidino group. This represents the direct guanidinated pathway to the arginine precursor.

reactions.name: Photoreduction of β-aminopropionitrile to β-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation; H₂S serves as terminal reductant; operates on unreacted β-aminopropionitrile from the inefficient guanidination (rxn_008)

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling reduces the nitrile group of unreacted β-aminopropionitrile to an aldehyde via a thioamide intermediate: (1) H₂S adds across the C≡N to form a thioamide, (2) UV/Cu-mediated photoreduction converts the thioamide to an aldehyde (β-aminopropionaldehyde). The aldehyde is a reactive intermediate but is trapped immediately by residual cyanamide.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) identified that unreacted β-aminopropionitrile from the inefficient guanidination step is productively channeled through photoreduction to yield β-aminopropionaldehyde. This aldehyde is key to the formation of the hemiaminal 37 intermediate. The Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox mechanism with H₂S is central to the cyanosulfidic framework.

reactions.name: Hemiaminal 37 formation by cyanamide trapping of aldehyde

conditions.other: Spontaneous reaction; described as 'extraordinarily efficient' by Patel et al.; pH neutral to slightly basic

reactions.mechanism: The amine nitrogen of cyanamide attacks the carbonyl carbon of β-aminopropionaldehyde, forming a stable hemiaminal adduct (designated compound 37 in Patel et al., 2015). The hemiaminal is thermodynamically stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding and the electron-withdrawing cyanamide nitrogen, preventing the aldehyde from participating in destructive side reactions.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) identified hemiaminal 37 as a critical intermediate that 'traps' the reactive aldehyde and channels it toward productive synthesis. The formation is described as 'extraordinarily efficient,' making it a kinetically and thermodynamically favored step that prevents aldehyde degradation in the complex prebiotic mixture.

reactions.name: Kiliani-Fischer homologation of hemiaminal 37 to arginine α-aminonitrile

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm); sequential dark (HCN addition) and light (photoreduction) steps; H₂S as reductant

reactions.mechanism: Hemiaminal 37 undergoes Kiliani-Fischer homologation analogous to rxn_009: (1) HCN addition to the masked aldehyde generates a cyanohydrin, (2) H₂S sulfidolysis yields an α-hydroxythioamide, (3) UV photodeoxygenation removes the hydroxyl, (4) final photoreductive amination with NH₃ and HCN produces the arginine α-aminonitrile. The cyanamide component of the hemiaminal provides the guanidino group in the final product.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) showed that hemiaminal 37, through the Kiliani-Fischer homologation sequence, converges on the same arginine α-aminonitrile product as the direct guanidination route (rxn_009). This convergence from two parallel sub-pathways is a hallmark of the cyanosulfidic systems chemistry approach, providing redundancy and robustness to the synthesis.

reactions.name: Hydrolytic conversion of arginine α-aminonitrile to L-arginine

conditions.other: Mild aqueous conditions; slow hydrolysis at neutral pH or accelerated at mildly acidic pH; geothermal pool cycling may enhance rate

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of the arginine α-aminonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first to an amide (argininamide) and then to the free carboxylic acid (arginine). Under mildly acidic or neutral aqueous conditions, this proceeds through water addition to the C≡N bond.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids is a well-established step in prebiotic amino acid synthesis, forming the basis of the Strecker synthesis completion (Miller, 1957; Patel et al., 2015). The hydrolysis can proceed abiotically under geothermal pool conditions. This step converts the cyanosulfidic α-aminonitrile product to the biologically relevant free amino acid.

reactions.name: HCN polymerization on montmorillonite under UV

conditions.other: Concentrated HCN solution (>0.1 M); UV irradiation initiates radical polymerization; montmorillonite surface concentrates HCN monomers; mildly basic pH (NH₃ present)

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes base-catalyzed oligomerization: (1) HCN dimerizes to iminoacetonitrile, (2) further addition gives the trimer aminomalononitrile, (3) the tetramer diaminomaleonitrile (DAMN) forms, (4) continued polymerization produces heterogeneous HCN oligomers with embedded amino acid and heterocyclic substructures. Montmorillonite concentrates HCN and templates ordered oligomerization.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization is one of the oldest studied prebiotic reactions (Oró, 1961; Ferris & Hagan, 1984). The oligomers incorporate nitrogen-rich substructures including diaminoacids. Montmorillonite catalysis of HCN oligomerization has been studied by Ferris et al. (2005). TiO₂ photocatalysis may assist radical initiation.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomers to ornithine

conditions.other: Acidic hydrolysis (pH 2-4) at moderate temperature; geothermal pool environment; prolonged exposure (days to weeks); yields a complex amino acid mixture from which ornithine is one product

reactions.mechanism: Acidic or thermal hydrolysis cleaves nitrile, amidine, and amide bonds within HCN oligomers, releasing free amino acids. Ornithine (2,5-diaminopentanoic acid) is produced from oligomer substructures that contain five-carbon diamine-bearing fragments. The hydrolysis is non-selective, yielding glycine, alanine, aspartate, and diaminoacids including ornithine and 2,4-diaminobutyric acid.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris & Hagan (1984) demonstrated that acid hydrolysis of HCN polymers releases a diverse set of amino acids including ornithine. Ornithine has also been identified in the Murchison meteorite (Pizzarello & Shock, 2010) and in spark-discharge experiments (Johnson et al., 2008), confirming its prebiotic availability through multiple routes.

reactions.name: Guanidination of ornithine by cyanamide to arginine

conditions.other: Neutral to slightly alkaline pH (7-9); room temperature; proceeds over hours to days; highly selective for the δ-amino group of ornithine over α-amino group due to lower steric hindrance

reactions.mechanism: The δ-amino group (side chain primary amine) of ornithine attacks the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide, forming a guanidine linkage through nucleophilic addition. A proton transfer follows to yield the stable guanidinium group of arginine. The reaction is selective: the side chain amine is more nucleophilic and less sterically hindered than the α-amino group, favoring δ-guanidination.
reactions.reasoning: Recent studies have demonstrated that ornithine residues in polypeptides can be selectively converted to arginine by aqueous cyanamide at room temperature (Radakovic et al., 2022). This post-synthetic guanidination is proposed as the prebiotic route by which arginine entered the amino acid alphabet: ornithine-containing statistical polypeptides were chemically modified to arginine-containing proteins with enhanced DNA-binding and α-helix-forming properties. The reaction also proceeds on free ornithine monomers.

reactions.name: Reductive homologation of β-aminopropionitrile to ornithine

conditions.other: UV irradiation; H₂S as reductant; sequential Kiliani-Fischer homologation without guanidination; followed by nitrile hydrolysis in situ

reactions.mechanism: β-Aminopropionitrile undergoes Kiliani-Fischer chain extension analogous to the arginine pathway but without cyanamide guanidination: (1) Cu/UV photoreduction of nitrile to aldehyde with H₂S, (2) HCN addition to extend the carbon chain by one, (3) repeat homologation to reach C5, (4) final reductive amination with NH₃ introduces the δ-amino group, (5) nitrile hydrolysis yields free ornithine. This non-guanidinated homologation channel produces the same C5 diaminoacid scaffold as HCN oligomer hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: The Kiliani-Fischer homologation chemistry of the cyanosulfidic framework (Patel et al., 2015) can extend carbon chains without guanidination when cyanamide is absent or consumed. This provides an alternative ornithine synthesis route through the same photoredox machinery used for the arginine pathway. The parallel operation of guanidinated and non-guanidinated channels from the same β-aminopropionitrile starting point is consistent with the systems chemistry paradigm where product diversity arises from reagent stoichiometry.

pathways.name: Direct cyanosulfidic via guanidination (canonical Patel pathway)
pathways.description: The direct cyanosulfidic route: acetylene and HCN form acrylonitrile, ammonia addition yields β-aminopropionitrile, cyanamide guanidination installs the guanidino group, and Kiliani-Fischer homologation produces the arginine α-aminonitrile, which is hydrolyzed to arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic via hemiaminal 37 trapping (parallel Patel pathway)
pathways.description: The parallel cyanosulfidic route where unreacted β-aminopropionitrile is photoreduced to β-aminopropionaldehyde, trapped by cyanamide as hemiaminal 37, and then undergoes Kiliani-Fischer homologation to the same arginine α-aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal sourcing with guanidinated cyanosulfidic pathway
pathways.description: Complete pathway from CO₂ and H₂ through hydrothermal formate and formaldehyde to HCN, then the surface cyanosulfidic guanidination route to arginine. Demonstrates Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical environmental flow.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal sourcing with hemiaminal 37 pathway
pathways.description: Complete pathway from hydrothermal CO₂ reduction to HCN, through the hemiaminal 37 branch of the cyanosulfidic pathway. Parallel to P3 but through the aldehyde-trapping route.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal cyanamide convergent with guanidinated pathway
pathways.description: Cyanamide is sourced from hydrothermal urea synthesis and thermal dehydration, converging with the surface cyanosulfidic pathway at the guanidination step. Both HCN (exogenous) and cyanamide (hydrothermal) feed into the surface reactions.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis to ornithine then guanidination
pathways.description: HCN polymerizes on mineral surfaces under UV, and the resulting oligomers are hydrolyzed to release ornithine, which is then guanidinated by cyanamide to yield arginine. A completely independent route from the cyanosulfidic pathway.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal HCN to oligomers to ornithine to arginine
pathways.description: Full Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical pathway: hydrothermal C1 fixation produces HCN, which polymerizes at the surface, hydrolyzes to ornithine in geothermal pools, and is guanidinated to arginine.

pathways.name: β-Aminopropionitrile homologation to ornithine then guanidination
pathways.description: β-Aminopropionitrile from the cyanosulfidic pathway is diverted through non-guanidinated Kiliani-Fischer homologation to yield ornithine, which is then guanidinated to arginine. This pathway branches from the cyanosulfidic route at the β-aminopropionitrile hub.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal HCN with β-aminopropionitrile homologation
pathways.description: Complete pathway from hydrothermal CO₂ reduction through HCN, acrylonitrile, β-aminopropionitrile, then homologation to ornithine and guanidination. Demonstrates how hydrothermal C1 feeds into the full ornithine-to-arginine route.

pathways.name: Dual hydrothermal convergent pathway (HCN + cyanamide both hydrothermal)
pathways.description: Both key feedstocks—HCN and cyanamide—are sourced from hydrothermal chemistry. HCN (from CO₂ reduction) feeds β-aminopropionitrile homologation to ornithine, while cyanamide (from urea dehydration) provides the guanidinating agent. Both converge at the ornithine guanidination step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Canonical Patel cyanosulfidic pathway. Strongest literature support. Starts directly from Surface environment so lacks the Hydrothermal → Surface environmental flow, but the core chemistry is excellent. rxn_008 guanidinylation is described in literature as 'relatively inefficient' but acknowledged. rxn_009 is complex multi-reagent Kiliani-Fischer homologation but well-documented. rxn_013 hydrolysis to L-arginine specifically (not racemic) is not fully justified—chirality selection not addressed.
suggestions: Address enantioselectivity mechanism for L-arginine production
Consider specifying UV wavelength ranges for photoredox steps in rxn_009

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Parallel Patel pathway via hemiaminal 37 trapping. rxn_011 is described in the literature as 'extraordinarily efficient,' making this the more kinetically favored route. The photoreduction of β-aminopropionitrile to β-aminopropionaldehyde (rxn_010) via H₂S is mechanistically sound within the cyanosulfidic framework. Same chirality concern as P1. Slightly preferred over P1 due to the high selectivity of the hemiaminal trapping step.
suggestions: This is likely the kinetically dominant pathway—consider labeling it as the primary route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Full environmental progression Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical. rxn_001 (CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite) is well-supported. rxn_002 (formate to formaldehyde) is thermodynamically challenging but plausible under hydrothermal conditions. rxn_003 (formaldehyde + NH₃ → HCN on pyrite) is the weakest link—this requires an oxidative dehydrogenation step that pyrite catalysis at high temperature can partially justify, but the mechanism is not cleanly established. Strong downstream cyanosulfidic chemistry compensates.
suggestions: Consider alternative HCN sources (e.g., atmospheric photolysis of CH₄/N₂) to bypass rxn_002-003
Provide more mechanistic detail for rxn_003

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Full hydrothermal sourcing combined with the kinetically superior hemiaminal 37 pathway. Inherits the strengths of P2's downstream chemistry and the environmental completeness of P3. Same concerns about rxn_002 and rxn_003 hydrothermal HCN generation. The 9-step length is realistic for a multi-environment prebiotic scenario.
suggestions: Same as P3—consider alternative HCN sourcing

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Nicely demonstrates convergence of hydrothermal cyanamide (via urea) with cyanosulfidic core pathway. rxn_004 (urea from NH₃ + CO₂) is thermodynamically feasible at hydrothermal pressures. rxn_005 (urea dehydration to cyanamide) is well-known but listed as Surface—should specify mineral agent (e.g., silica or phosphate minerals). Good convergent design showing how separate hydrothermal and surface sub-networks feed into the same pathway.
suggestions: Specify mineral catalyst for rxn_005 Surface dehydration step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short alternative pathway via HCN oligomers. rxn_014 (HCN polymerization on montmorillonite) is well-supported. rxn_015 (HCN oligomer hydrolysis to ornithine) is the main weakness—HCN oligomer hydrolysis yields a complex mixture of amino acids, and selectivity for ornithine specifically is low and not well-characterized quantitatively. rxn_016 (ornithine guanidination by cyanamide) is strongly supported by recent literature. Pathway lacks Hydrothermal contribution (starts at Surface).
suggestions: Acknowledge low selectivity of rxn_015 for ornithine
Add hydrothermal HCN generation steps to complete environmental flow

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Full environmental chain from hydrothermal CO₂ through HCN oligomers to ornithine to arginine. Good environmental progression but compounds the weaknesses of rxn_002/003 (HCN generation) with rxn_015 (ornithine selectivity from oligomers). The guanidination final step (rxn_016) is strong. Overall a viable alternative route but lower confidence than cyanosulfidic pathways.
suggestions: Provide experimental yield data for ornithine from HCN oligomer hydrolysis if available

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Bridges the cyanosulfidic and ornithine routes via reductive homologation of β-aminopropionitrile to ornithine (rxn_017). This is conceptually sound within the cyanosulfidic framework but rxn_017 lacks specific experimental precedent for ornithine production. The conversion of a 3-carbon aminonitrile to a 5-carbon amino acid in one step requires significant chain extension. The guanidination step is excellent.
suggestions: Break rxn_017 into more explicit mechanistic steps
Specify the photoreduction/homologation conditions more precisely

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Full hydrothermal sourcing feeding into the β-aminopropionitrile homologation route. Good environmental progression but inherits weaknesses from both rxn_002/003 and rxn_017. Represents a plausible alternative but with multiple uncertain steps.
suggestions: Strengthen rxn_017 with more mechanistic detail

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Most comprehensive pathway showing dual hydrothermal convergence (both HCN and cyanamide sourced from hydrothermal environments). Excellent environmental progression. The convergence of two separate hydrothermal product streams at the surface cyanosulfidic chemistry is conceptually elegant and aligns with the 'systems chemistry' paradigm emphasized in the literature. Same rxn_002/003 concerns but otherwise strong.
suggestions: This pathway best demonstrates the network's convergent architecture

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an excellent synthesis network for L-Arginine that closely aligns with the state-of-the-art literature on prebiotic arginine synthesis. The network successfully implements both the Patel/Sutherland cyanosulfidic pathway (with both guanidination and hemiaminal 37 sub-routes) and the ornithine guanidination route, creating meaningful convergence and redundancy. The five hub molecules (HCN, cyanamide, β-aminopropionitrile, ornithine, arginine α-aminonitrile) are well-chosen and create a densely interconnected topology. Key strengths: (1) Direct implementation of the experimentally validated Patel et al. cyanosulfidic chemistry including the hemiaminal 37 intermediate; (2) Incorporation of the ornithine → arginine post-synthetic modification route as discussed in recent literature; (3) Good Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical environmental flow; (4) Appropriate mineral catalysts specified (greigite, magnetite, pyrite, Cu(I), montmorillonite). Key weaknesses: (1) rxn_003 (formaldehyde + NH₃ → HCN on pyrite) has questionable mechanism—oxidative dehydration is not straightforward; (2) rxn_015 (HCN oligomer → ornithine) lacks selectivity justification; (3) rxn_017 (β-aminopropionitrile → ornithine) is a large mechanistic leap in one step; (4) Chirality/enantioselectivity not addressed anywhere; (5) rxn_005 (Surface) should specify mineral agent. The literature alignment is very strong—the network captures the key insights from the deep research including cyanosulfidic systems chemistry, Cu(I) photoredox catalysis, the role of cyanamide as a universal reagent, and the ornithine-first hypothesis.
